era_context: Han Dynasty under Emperor Wu is at its height of military and territorial expansion. Parthian Empire is consolidating dominance in the Near East under Mithridates I. Rome is the dominant power in the Mediterranean. This is a period of major geopolitical realignment, with three major imperial powers emerging as dominant regional forces. The early Silk Road trade networks are beginning to form.

events.title: Zhang Qian's return from Central Asia (third alternative dating)
events.description: Some modern scholars date Zhang Qian's return from his second Central Asian mission to 125 BCE. This mission brought back detailed geographical and political intelligence about the Western Regions, the Parthian Empire, and the routes to India. The exact dating of Zhang Qian's return remains one of the most disputed chronological problems in Han Dynasty history, with proposed dates ranging from 129 to 125 BCE.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled early 1st century BCE
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu, 1st century CE

events.certainty_note: The exact year of Zhang Qian's return is highly disputed. Different scholars propose 129, 126, or 125 BCE based on different interpretations of the chronological data in Han sources. No contemporary documentation of the exact return date survives. This represents a fundamental historiographic problem for this period.
cross_references: See -129-001 and -126-001 for alternative datings of the same event

events.title: Han Dynasty military campaigns and Xiongnu conflicts
events.description: Emperor Wu of Han continues military campaigns against the Xiongnu confederation. These campaigns are part of a broader strategy to secure the northern frontier and open trade routes to Central Asia. The Han military superiority in this period gradually shifts the balance of power on the steppe, though the Xiongnu remain a formidable threat. Large cavalry forces and logistical innovations characterize Han military operations.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled early 1st century BCE
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu, 1st century CE

events.certainty_note: Han military campaigns against the Xiongnu were continuous during Emperor Wu's reign (141–87 BCE). Specific events in 125 BCE are not individually documented in surviving sources.

events.title: Parthian Empire consolidation under Mithridates I
events.description: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE) continues to consolidate Parthian control over Mesopotamia and the Levant. By 125 BCE, the Parthian Empire has become the dominant power in the Near East, rivaling the Seleucid Empire and blocking direct Han-Roman contact. Parthian expansion and consolidation during this period fundamentally reshape the geopolitical landscape of Asia.
key_figures: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE)

sources.name: Strabo, Geography, Book 11 and 16 (1st century BCE/CE, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: Mithridates I's reign dates and general territorial expansion are known from numismatic evidence and later Greek and Roman sources. The exact chronology of his conquests remains debated.

events.title: Roman consolidation in the Mediterranean and North Africa
events.description: Rome continues to consolidate its control over the Mediterranean and North Africa following the destruction of Carthage (146 BCE) and the annexation of Macedonia (148 BCE). By 125 BCE, Rome is the dominant military and political power in the region. Roman influence over the fragmenting Seleucid Empire and the independent Greek city-states is growing, though formal annexation of some territories (such as Syria) is still decades away.
key_figures: Roman Senate and magistrates (names not consistently recorded for this specific year)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (books now lost for this period, but referenced in later epitomes)

events.certainty_note: Roman expansion in the Mediterranean during the 130s–120s BCE is well-attested in general terms, but specific events in 125 BCE are not documented in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Zhang Qian's return is highly disputed among modern scholars. Proposed dates range from 129 to 125 BCE, with no consensus. The surviving Han sources (Shiji and Han Shu) do not provide a clear, unambiguous date. Additionally, the significance attributed to Zhang Qian's mission as the 'beginning of the Silk Road' is somewhat anachronistic—the formal trade networks took centuries to develop, and Zhang Qian's primary mission was diplomatic, not commercial. The 'Silk Road' as a unified concept is a modern historiographic construct.
historiographic_note: 125 BCE is poorly documented. The historical record for this year is almost entirely dependent on later compilations (Han Shu, Shiji, Livy's epitomes, Appian) written decades or centuries after the events. No contemporary written records from this year are known to survive. The dating of Zhang Qian's return is a key historiographic problem for this period, with different scholars proposing different years (129, 126, or 125 BCE) based on different interpretations of the source material. Archaeological evidence for this specific year is minimal. The historical record is heavily biased toward Han Dynasty sources and Mediterranean (Greco-Roman) perspectives; other regions and civilizations are essentially invisible.

graph_edges.note: Alternative dating of Zhang Qian's return; same event, different proposed year

Han military campaigns and Parthian consolidation are contemporaneous geopolitical processes
